featured.title: Drop Mech
featured.description: An actively stabilized aerial delivery system that autonomously lowers and deploys payloads from a drone at 150ft altitude. Built for the SUAS 2026 competition.

detail.overview: Drop Mech is a winch-and-droid payload delivery system designed for the SUAS Robonation 2026 competition. A hexacopter drone lowers a tethered "droid" platform via a winch, which autonomously stabilizes itself during descent using onboard sensors and PID-controlled diagonal propellers. Once it detects the ground, it releases the payload and retracts.
highlights: 60.3s drop cycle time (requirement: <80s)
Droid weight: 0.9 lbs (requirement: <2 lbs)
Ground detection range: 13ft (requirement: >10ft)
Custom PCB designed in Altium and manufactured via JLCPCB
Extended Kalman Filter fuses BMI088 IMU and VL53L1X ToF sensor data
XBee wireless communication (IEEE 802.15.4) between drone and droid
Demonstrated at the College of Engineering Design Expo
detail.role: Embedded systems development — firmware for the STM32 microcontroller, sensor fusion pipeline, PID tuning, and PCB bring-up.

featured.title: MiSpace Ice Forecast
featured.description: A machine learning ice condition forecasting system with a web frontend for visualizing Arctic predictions. Won 2nd place ($10K) at MiSpace 2025.

detail.overview: Built at the Michigan Office of Defense & Aerospace Innovation hackathon, this platform predicts Arctic ice conditions using machine learning models with hyperparameter tuning. A TypeScript web frontend lets users explore forecast results interactively.
highlights: Won 2nd place and $10,000 prize
ML pipeline with hyperparameter tuning for ice prediction models
Interactive web-based visualization frontend
Processed satellite and environmental data for training
detail.role: Full-stack development — built the frontend visualization layer and integrated the ML prediction pipeline.

featured.title: Bayesian Localization
featured.description: A comparative benchmark of three recursive Bayesian estimation algorithms for robot localization, tested on a simulated PR2 robot with a kidnapped robot challenge.

detail.overview: This project benchmarks three recursive Bayesian estimation algorithms — Particle Filter, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), and Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) — for robot localization. A simulated PR2 robot navigates a figure-8 trajectory in PyBullet, using 2D LiDAR readings to localize itself. The "Kidnapped Robot" challenge tests each filter's ability to recover from sudden displacement.
highlights: Particle Filter achieved 0.68m RMSE — best among the three
Only the Particle Filter recovered from the kidnapped robot scenario without manual re-initialization
3,000 particles used for the Particle Filter implementation
Full simulation environment built in PyBullet with PR2 URDF model
Quantitative comparison across position RMSE, heading error, and recovery time
detail.role: Algorithm implementation and benchmarking — implemented all three filters, built the simulation harness, and ran comparative analysis.

featured.title: wubwub
featured.description: An interactive music visualizer that uses hand gestures to control playback and audio effects. Won grand prize at BigRed Hacks 2025.

detail.overview: wubwub is a gesture-controlled music visualizer that lets you manipulate audio in real time using hand movements. MediaPipe tracks hand landmarks through a webcam, an Arduino provides haptic feedback, and a Flask backend processes audio effects. The React frontend renders real-time visualizations that respond to both the music and your gestures.
highlights: Won grand prize at BigRed Hacks 2025
Real-time hand gesture recognition via MediaPipe
Audio effect processing through Flask backend
Arduino integration for haptic feedback
Responsive music visualizations built in React
detail.role: Full-stack — built the React visualizer frontend, integrated MediaPipe hand tracking, and connected the audio processing pipeline.

featured.title: Pi-Pal
featured.description: A gesture-controlled hospital room system powered by computer vision and demo-ed with a Raspberry Pi. Won grand prize at Hoya Hacks 2025.

detail.overview: Pi-Pal is a hands-free hospital room control system designed for patients with limited mobility. A Raspberry Pi with a camera uses MediaPipe and OpenCV to recognize hand gestures, letting patients control room features — lights, TV, nurse call — without touching anything. A Next.js dashboard provides a web interface for configuration and monitoring.
highlights: Won grand prize at Hoya Hacks 2025
Gesture recognition via MediaPipe + OpenCV on Raspberry Pi
Next.js web dashboard for room control and monitoring
MongoDB backend for patient preferences and room state
Designed for accessibility in hospital environments
detail.role: Computer vision pipeline — built the gesture recognition system on the Raspberry Pi and connected it to the web dashboard.
